summary: DHH reports that 37signals' cloud exit has already reduced their cloud spending by 60%, from $180,000/month to under $80,000/month, yielding approximately $1 million per year in savings. The hardware investment of about $500,000 in new servers will pay for itself in less than six months. He projects eventual savings could reach $2 million per year, or $10 million over five years. He argues this isn't an anomaly, pointing to Snapchat's $3 billion cloud spend as evidence that many companies are massively overpaying. The savings were delayed because reserved instance contracts had to expire first, but the numbers are now materializing as predicted.
key_points: Cloud spending dropped 60% from ~$180K/month to under $80K/month after migrating applications off AWS
The $500K hardware investment will pay for itself in under six months from monthly savings alone
Projected long-term savings could reach $2M/year or $10M over five years
Reserved instance contracts delayed the full savings realization, but the remaining spend will phase out through the year
The post-bubble era means profitability matters again, making cloud cost optimization newly urgent for many companies
money_quotes: The only way to get pricing in the cloud down from obscene to merely offensive is through reserved instances.
By the basic comparison of money saved vs money spent, we'll be in the money on the big purchase with the current monthly savings in less than six months. That's just astounding!
We don't have to squint hard to see the eventual savings climb all the way up to about $2m/year. That would be TEN MILLION DOLLARS over five years.
From having looked at the unoptimized cloud bills at other software companies, our savings may in fact be modest compared to what's possible.
Don't tell me there wasn't a billion or so in potential savings on that tab.
That didn't used to matter, because who cared if the business was profitable or not, but now it sorta does!
key_insight: Leaving the cloud yielded a 60% cost reduction that will pay back the hardware investment in under six months, vindicating the case that owning your own infrastructure is dramatically cheaper for companies with predictable workloads.
